# Soda of the Week: Olipop tastes like too much and not enough at the same time

---

Christian D'Andrea | USA TODAY Sports

Welcome back to FTW’s Beverage of the Week series. Here, we mostly chronicle and review beers, but happily expand that scope to any beverage that pairs well with sports. Yes, even cookie dough whiskey.

I write about sports for a living. As such, I drink a stupid amount of diet soda.

This is part of the job, like having opinions on Bruce Springsteen or, as of 2023, knowing things about Taylor Swift. Every press box or media workroom you've ever seen will begin with a healthy stash of zero-calorie sodas, only to see it run out long before the regular Cokes dwindle.

Fortunately, we're in a whole era of diet soda glory. Sure that applies to your Sprites Zero and the like, but there's a swell of smaller companies pushing their way to the forefront, promising big flavors and modest calorie counts. Last week, I talked about United Sodas of America. Today I'm drinking Olipop.

Olipop had been on my radar for a while, though its $2 per can asking price at my local Woodman's effectively kept me away. But since non-alcoholic beverages are making a real push for consideration this summer, it felt like as good a time as any to see what Olipop brings to the table and if it's worth the price.

## Classic Grape: B

It pours like carbonated Welch's grape juice and smells like concord grape. That comes through in the first sip; it's thick for a soda, heavier than what you'd expect and delivering on the promise of real juice on the can.

There's a minor tartness to the opening sip, which quickly evolves into low-key sweetness. The end result leans into the juiciness with a little bit of Stevia sugar. That leaves an overall complex feeling and density to what's traditionally been a thin, one-note soda.

That's not a slight on typical grape sodas; I like that simple, way-sweet Fanta feel. This is just the grown-up version of that.

Anyway, I might be an unreliable narrator on these thanks to my grownup taste buds. I enlisted the help of my six-year-old daughter to add her perspective to the mix.

Quinn's Review: It's still good but just still too kinda fizzy. I'm gonna put it here so it gets less fizzy. I just don't like it because it tastes too bubbly.

## Vintage Cola: C-

It pours fizzy and smells like caramel and vanilla off the top -- a good start for any cola. The first sip brings out more of those spices in a slightly overpowering way. This is a cola that tastes like a Christmas cookie, with cinnamon and clove and that aforementioned vanilla swirling together.

It's very different from Coke or even other craft colas (Sprecher Puma

➝ Review By: Dan Scalco

|

➝

Bottom Line:

These drinks are the closest soda alternatives I've ever tasted. The one thing holding them back from being almost identical is that they don't have as much carbonation as the real thing. However, they're packed with plant-based ingredients, prebiotics and fiber, to create a gut-healthy drink backed by science.

Pros:

The taste comes very close to matching its soda counterpart.

They are more nutritionally friendly than diet sodas.

They offer a good variety of flavors with 9 currently available on their shop.

Cons:

There wasn't much carbonation or bubbles to the sodas.

It's priced higher than traditional sodas and diet sodas.

There are tons of "diet" sodas available these days. But what about sodas that taste like the real thing and are actually good for you?

That's where Olipop comes in. They market themselves as a better alternative to the sodas we all grew up drinking (think old school cola and root beer).

When I first heard of Olipop I was skeptical. I've tried my fair share of low-sugar and low-calorie soft drinks—all of which I thought never really tasted like their traditional counterparts.

However, when I heard that Olipop was infused with plant fiber, prebiotics, and other botanicals, I was intrigued. Could this truly be a better alternative to the sugar-loaded sodas I'd given up long ago?

There was only one way to find out...

In this review I'll walk you through my experience trying Olipop—what I enjoyed, what I didn't, and if it's actually worth it.

## What Is Olipop?

Olipop Ginger Lemon

Olipop is canned soda (12 oz) that uses alternative plant-based ingredients to mimic the tastes of traditional, classic sodas.

Each can has about 32% of your daily recommended fiber, no artificial sweeteners such as aspartame, and supports digestive health through the use of prebiotics.

## How Olipop Got Started

Olipop is the brainchild of founding partners Ben Goodwin and David Lester.

While it m

results.title: Brutally Honest OLIPOP Review *Read Before Buying* - Vitality Vixens
results.author: Erin Zack
results.text:
Brutally Honest OLIPOP Review *Read Before Buying* - Vitality Vixens

We believe that you deserve to know the honest, unfiltered truths about certain health & fitness fads. Today we’re going to be breaking down a popular soda alternative that we’d been seeing all over the dang place – Olipop. I swear, I couldn’t go a minute on Instagram or YouTube without seeing someone talking about these sodas. But when I went to the website and saw that it was $40 for a 12-pack, I was a little shook on how that could be worth it… So Mary & I decided to try out all 6 flavors for y’all (so you don’t have to) and break it down in this full Olipop review!

## The Olipop Review

We wanted to hop on here to answer a few initial questions we had about Olipop before buying. So scroll down to see the answers to:

1. Is Olipop really healthy?
2. Is there caffeine in Olipop?
3. Does Olipop make you poop? 💩

If you want to see a breakdown of all 6 flavors (Which ones were good? Which ones sucked?) – watch our full video review below.

If you enjoy the video & want to see more like it, be sure to like and subscribe to our channel! We love seeing you guys over on YouTube and chatting with you in the comments.

Disclaimer: We’re not doctors or dietitians, and the information in this article is just based on our personal experience. It should not be used in place of medical/nutrition advice from a professional.

## Is Olipop really healthy?

Olipop is advertised as a “sparkling tonic” that helps with digestive health. This claim is based on Olipop having prebiotics, 9 grams of fiber, and “botanical extracts” (1).

Personally, I didn’t experience any digestive boost by drinking Olipop. My digestion is already pretty good to begin with, so it could be more beneficial for people with a lower fiber diet.

Each can has under 6 grams of sugar, which compared to typical sodas (ranging from 30-50 grams of sugar) is definitely an improvement.

Personally, I thought most of the flavors tasted pretty good and didn’t taste like artificial sweetener or heavily Stevia induced. I hate the taste of natural sweetener (Stevia, etc.) when it’s overpowering, but I didn’t notice that in most of these flavors.

(To see our full breakdown of each of the flavors, be sure to watch the video above!)

Overall, compared to a regular soda, Olipop is definitely a healthier option. But if you’re craving a nice, carbonated soda beverage, Olipop may not fully satisfy your craving. In both Mary’s & my opinions, the carbonation factor was seriously lacking in these.

## Is there caffeine in Olipop?

The only Olipop flavor that contains caffeine is the Vintage Cola, with 50mg of caffeine from green tea (1). This is pretty comparable to other sodas (Diet Coke: 46mg; Dr. Pepper: 41mg; Coke: 34mg) (2).

I was disappointed at first that most of the flavors of Olipop didn’t contain caffeine. However, it made it easier for late evening/night drinking when you don’t want to be caffeinated before bed.

Personally, 50m

## Reviews mention

OLIPOP earns largely positive reviews as a convincing soda alternative that satisfies fizzy cravings and, for many, feels easier on digestion; standouts like Vintage Cola, Classic Root Beer, Strawberry Vanilla, Classic Grape, Orange Squeeze, Ginger Lemon, and Tropical Punch draw frequent praise. Reviews also flag notable weaknesses: a pronounced stevia/alternative‑sweetener aftertaste, occasional flavors that taste artificial or “medicinal,” light carbonation that can seem flat, and divided opinions on gut‑health effects and sweetness levels.

results.title: Olipop Lemon Lime: An Honest Review | Sporked
results.author: Gwynedd Stuart
results.text:
Olipop Lemon Lime: An Honest Review | Sporked 

 

 

# An Honest Review of New Olipop Lemon Lime

 

Credit: Liv Averett / Olipop

Copy this link to share with your friends!

`
 https://sporked.com/article/olipop-lemon-lime-review/ `

 

Olipop may be a “a new kind of soda,” but the brand has taken a lot of cues from the classic soda world when it comes to flavors. Their lineup of better-for-you prebiotic, less-sugar bevs has included grape, orange, cola, cherry cola, root beer, cream soda—the list goes on. For a long time, though, there was a pretty obvious omission: lemon lime, arguably the second most popular soda flavor behind cola. In February, the brand finally fixed that when it released Lemon Lime on its site and at Whole Foods stores. But will Olipop fans want to stock up on this new flavor? And should lemon lime soda fans give Olipop a shot?

## Videos by Sporked

---

New Product!

Olipop Lemon Lime

Welp, it looks like we might need to update our ranking of the best Olipop flavors, because this one is good. But, right away, there’s a caveat: It doesn’t really taste like lemon lime soda. It’s delicious, but it tastes like a carbonated lemon iced tea (it reminded me of an Arizona Lemon Tea with some fizz). I didn’t pick up the flavor of lime at all. I think people who buy Olipop because they want a (way) lower-sugar alternative to Sprite or Starry might be disappointed, but if you’re willing to appreciate the flavor for what it is, there’s nothing to dislike.

Also, if you’re a soda person and you’re reading this (hi) and you’re new to Olipop, don’t think that less sugar means that these fall into seltzer territory. Olipops may be lower sugar, but they’re still plenty sweet, thanks to a blend of cassava root and other alternative sweeteners. It’s a carbonated drink soda fans can still get behind—as long as they don’t have their hearts set on that classic lemon lime flavor they know and love.

Credit: Liv Averett / Olipop

Rating:

8.5/10

Sporks

results.title: We Tried Every Olipop Flavor: Here's How They Ranked
results.author: EMMA GINSBERG
results.text:
We Tried Every Olipop Flavor: Here's How They Ranked

­

--

# We Tried Every Olipop Flavor We Could Get Our Hands On: Here’s How They Ranked

July 19, 2024

written by EMMA GINSBERG

Here in the hallowed halls of The Everygirl, taste test rankings are a time-honored tradition. You might be familiar with our annual Starbucks fall and holiday drinks rankings, where we rank each seasonal Starbucks drop (as is our journalistic duty, of course). However, we know that some prefer a different drink for their daily little treat: soda.

Enter The Everygirl’s first-ever soda (or pop) tasting, dedicated to our girls who love a sparkling bevvy sitting on their desk at all times. While you probably already have your own personal favorite of the name-brand sodas, there are so many alternatives out there today that we knew we had to rank them. For this ranking, we opted for Olipop: a better-for-you soda that comes in 17 different flavors and boasts prebiotics, fiber, and botanical extracts in its ingredients list.

Now, if you’re a Diet Coke lover through and through, by all means, stick to your fave. And if you refuse to drink“healthy” soda alternatives because they aren’t really that healthy, good for you, queen! But for those of us who keep our fridges stocked with alternative soda and delusion, we gathered as many Olipop flavors as we could possibly find and diligently tasted them all.

## How We Ranked Them

Our eight taste testers tried 11 Olipop flavors and scored each drink on a scale of 1-5 in the categories of “overall taste” and “likelihood to get again.” This means that there is a total of 10 possible points per drink from each taste-tester, and each drink has a maximum of 80 possible points. Then, we averaged each score to get our final rating out of 10. Without further ado, here are the best Olipop flavors, ranked.

## 11. Cream Soda

“It was like drinking a Crumbl cookie.”

### Score: 0.8/10

While sipping a cream soda sounds kind of romantic, our team had no choice but to rank this flavor dead last. You’ll notice that a consistent complaint throughout this ranking is that some of the flavors are simply too sweet to really enjoy, and this soda took the cake for the sweetest flavor. Abi, our social media assistant, aptly observed that drinking the cream soda Olipop was “like drinking a Crumbl cookie.” Paige, the branded content editor at The Everymom, felt that the cream soda tasted akin to “children’s toothpaste flavor.” You’re probably better off with an actual cookie.

## 10. Watermelon Lime

“The general consensus was that this drink smelled like rotten eggs.”

### Score: 1.1/10

Our team had high hopes for this flavor, but sadly, we were gravely disappointed. I won’t beat around the bush here: This soda has an absolutely rancid smell. While most of the taste testers agreed that the flavor was a true watermelon lime, the taste was masked by the bizarre scent. Jenna, The Everygirl’s editorial assistant, described the smell as “scary.” The gene

results.title: Olipop vs. Poppi - A Dietitian’s Review — Dietitian Hannah
results.author: Written By  Dietitian Hannah
results.text:
Olipop vs. Poppi - A Dietitian’s Review — Dietitian Hannah

# Olipop vs. Poppi - A Dietitian’s Review

Apr 29

DISCLAIMER: Links included in this blog might be affiliate links. Health with Hannah, LLC is a participant in the Amazon Services LLC Associates Program along with other affiliate programs. If you purchase a product or service with the links that I provide I may receive a small commission with no additional charge to you. Thank you for your support!

Prebiotic sodas have hit the market, two of the most popular brands being Olipop and Poppi. As a dietitian, I have been getting loads of questions about these beverages. Which is better? Are they a healthy swap for soda? Let’s break it down!

If you’re new here, hello and welcome! My name is Hannah and I am a non-diet dietitian and personal trainer here to help you improve your relationship with your food and exercise and finally find food freedom.

#### Table of contents:

What are prebiotics?

Olipop review

Poppi review

Does Olipop or Poppi have more fiber?

Are Olipop and Poppi good for gut health?

Which is healthier, Olipop or Poppi?

Are Olipop and Poppi healthier than soda?

### What are prebiotics?

Olipop and Poppi are categorized as “prebiotic sodas”. But what exactly does this mean? What even are prebiotics?

Prebiotics are a type of dietary fiber found in foods such as chicory root, garlic and onions, and bananas. All prebiotics are fiber, but not all fiber has prebiotic effects.

Prebiotics are not the same thing as probiotics, although both are important! Probiotics are not a type of fiber - they are living microorganisms that can help to maintain or improve the “good” gut bacteria. Essentially, prebiotics act as a food for the probiotics.

Olipop and Poppi contain a variety of prebiotic ingredients, which we will break down in the reviews below.

Related post: Are You Eating Enough Fiber?

### Olipop review

Olipop claims to be “a new kind of soda” that supports digestive health. Olipop comes in a variety of flavors including Orange Squeeze, Lemon Lime, Cream Soda, and many more.

#### Olipop nutrition facts

Olipop has 35-50 calories per 12-oz can. Each serving also provides 2-5 grams of sugar and 9 grams of dietary fiber. These beverages are not a source of protein or fat.

A few Olipop flavors contain caffeine from green tea, including Vintage Cola, Doctor Goodwin, and Cherry Cola.

#### Olipop ingredients

Olipop has a proprietary blend of ingredients called “ Olismart”, which includes cassava root fiber, chicory root inulin, Jerusalem artichoke inulin, nopal cactus extract, marshmallow root extract, calendula flower extract, and kudzu root extract. The sodas also include ingredients such as apple juice concentrate, stevia, and Himalayan pink salt.

The sources of prebiotic fiber in Olipop include the cassava root fiber, chicory root inulin, Jerusalem artichoke inulin, and kudzu root extract. These may help promote a healthy gut microbiome and improve digestion.

Olipop

results.title: Oli-Flop: A new soda OLIPOP is not worth the hype it has received and doesn’t replicate the taste of traditional soda | The Harbinger Online
results.author: Lucy Stephens
results.text:
Oli-Flop: A new soda OLIPOP is not worth the hype it has received and doesn’t replicate the taste of traditional soda | The Harbinger Online

Through its presence on TikTok and Instagram along with its appealing packaging seen in grocery stores, I familiarized myself with the brand OLIPOP — a healthier alternative to a normal, sugary soda. This soda cuts the calories of normal soda in half and supposedly strengthens gut health with added probiotics according to the OLIPOP website.

I avoided trying OLIPOPS for a while — I didn't want them to ruin the iconic tastes of my favorite sodas like Coke and Sprite. However, after hearing dozens of reviews claiming that the healthy drink tasted exactly like the traditional sodas, I became motivated to give them a try and possibly take my soda obsession to a healthier level.

The line of OLIPOP has 16 flavors with each one imitating a normal soda flavor. I decided to taste test three different flavors — Vintage Cola, Lemon Lime and Doctor Goodwin — assuming they would mimic the popular sodas, Coca-Cola, Sprite and Dr. Pepper.

Lucy Stephens| The Harbinger Online

Lucy Stephens| The Harbinger Online

Coca-Cola has been my go-to soda at any restaurant, movie or afternoon refresh and I was eager to see OLIPOP’s take on this iconic drink. For this flavor in particular, I had only heard good things about how it tasted exactly like Diet Coke. Sadly my hopes plummeted after just the first sip.

Upon cracking open the soda tab and taking the first sip, I wasn’t only unsatisfied, but confused. The overall flavor of the drink was incredibly fruity and seemed to fit in the realm of a cherry soda — not anywhere close to the traditional Cola taste.

The fizziness I was looking for in a can of Coke was nonexistent in the OLIPOP. Without the normal level of carbonation, the soda tasted extremely flat whereas a regular can of Coke’s carbonation almost burns the back of your throat — in a good way.

Maybe my expectations were too high in thinking I could replace my favorite soda with a healthy rebrand, but the OLIPOP Vintage Cola was nowhere close to what I expected.

Lucy Stephens| The Harbinger Online

Lucy Stephens| The Harbinger Online

After trying the Vintage Cola OLIPOP I was expecting the Doctor Goodwin flavor to taste similar, as a traditional Coke and Dr. Pepper often have a similar flavor.

The fruity flavor seen in the Vintage Cola came through in this soda as well — except the fruit flavor is more distinguishable as grape soda. The grape flavor was intense, which makes me question how this soda was recognized as Dr. Pepper-like.

Of the three sodas I tried, this one was my personal favorite — not because it is similar to the original soda, but because it tasted the most like a regular soda. I can enjoy a grape soda, but not when I’m expecting a Dr. Pepper replica.

Lucy Stephens| The Harbinger Online

Lucy Stephens| The Harbinger Online

I was skeptical about trying a lemon-lime soda with the amount of sugar cu

results.title: I've Curbed My Diet Coke Obsession With Olipop, a Healthier Soda
results.author: Marisa Petrarca
results.text:
OLIPOP Soda Review | PS Fitness

POPSUGAR Photography | Anvita ReddyPOPSUGAR Photography | Anvita Reddy

While each product featured is independently selected by our editors, we may include paid promotion. If you buy something through our links, we may earn commission. Read more about our Product Review Guidelines here.

I've been an avid Diet Coke drinker for as long as I can remember. But through the years, I've tried to intersperse my obsession with healthier alternatives. In all honesty, they usually didn't stick. Rarely would I try something that would impact me in a way that a cold bottle of DC would. That is until I got my hands on the social media-famous Olipop. Based in Oakland, California, and launched in 2018, the brand describes itself as a "new kind of soda." It was created to taste just like soda — and it does! — with that same sweet flavor and fizzy bubbles that many of us know and love. But what separates it from the pack, remarkably so, is that it's full of plant fiber and prebiotics to support your digestive and gut health. And here's the best part: The flavors are so good. (And did I mention the cans are cute?) Ahead, I break down everything you need to know about the brand and which flavors you need to scoop up stat.

## Is Olipop Healthy?

Olipop was in development for two years before it launched, cofounder David Lester told POPSUGAR. The hard work paid off in a better-for-you can of soda that's dietician-approved. Put simply, fiber and prebiotics — types of carbohydrates that we can't digest— found in these drinks feed the naturally occurring probiotics in our bodies that deal with digestion. This, in turn, may make the probiotics more effective and create a more balanced microbiome (read: the living bacteria in and on the human body).

"It is OLIPOP's mission to provide a real solution to consumers that want to benefit their digestive health, drink less soda, and share an accessible health product with their friends and family," cofounder Ben Goodwin wrote in a post explaining more about this process. For context, each 12-ounce can of Olipop contains 9 grams of fiber and 2-3 grams of sugar. In comparison, a 12-ounce can of Coke has virtually no dietary fiber and 39 grams of sugar. Wild, right?

So what's the verdict, health-wise? Should you be drinking a few of these daily? Simply put, Olipop is a "more healthy alternative than reaching for a can of soda," says Patrice Amore, MS, RD, LDN, clinical dietician and owner of Amore Fuels Nutrition. One thing to keep in mind, though, is if you already eat a high-fiber diet that hovers near the recommended 25 to 30 grams per day, you may want to restrict yourself to "one to two a day max" to avoid a "fiber overload."

## What I Like About Olipop

I love everything about Olipop, from the colorful packaging and ingredient transparency to the fun flavors and sweet taste (sans high sugar content). But if I had to pick a favorite it would be the flavors. I've tried all ten (twice) and
